name: Biology
examGuidance: Use VCE command verbs (identify, describe, explain, compare, evaluate) and standard biological terminology. Questions must be set in authentic contexts, requiring students to apply concepts to novel scenarios, interpret experimental data (including identifying errors, uncertainties, and outliers), and justify conclusions with evidence. Always specify units and define variables in experimental responses. Confine questions to the VCE Biology Study Design (2022–2026). Ensure mathematical components (e.g., genetic crosses, rate calculations) align with VCAA expected levels.
out_of_scope: Intermediate steps of Krebs Cycle or Calvin Cycle not specified in the Study Design
Detailed chemical structures of specific amino acids or nucleotides beyond general components
Non-human primate evolution beyond the hominin line (e.g., detailed lemur or tarsier phylogeny)
Specific drug brand names or proprietary medical technologies not mentioned in the Study Design
Plant hormones beyond those explicitly listed in the VCAA study design
Complex mathematical modeling of population genetics beyond simple allele frequency changes
Detailed steps of the complement cascade beyond general function
Specific names of all cytokines beyond general roles

technique_notes.core_concepts: Distinction between prokaryotic (lack membrane-bound organelles, circular DNA) and eukaryotic cells (membrane-bound organelles, linear DNA).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Focus on structural differences and the evolutionary significance of compartmentalisation in eukaryotes.
anti_prompts: Do not just list differences; explain how they impact cell function.

technique_notes.core_concepts: SA:V ratio as a limit to cell size and the driver for compartmentalisation and flattened cell shapes.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Require calculations of SA:V and linking results to efficiency of diffusion and waste removal.
anti_prompts: Do not forget that internal organelles also increase effective surface area for reactions.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Structure and function of chloroplasts, mitochondria, and other major organelles.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe how compartmentalisation allows for different environments (pH, enzyme concentration) within a single cell.
anti_prompts: Avoid simple recall; use scenarios where organelle malfunction affects the cell.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Fluid mosaic model: phospholipids, proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates. Role as a semi-permeable boundary.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the role of different components in maintaining fluidity and regulating transport.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse the roles of integral and peripheral proteins.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, and active transport (including bulk transport: endocytosis/exocytosis).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Distinguish between passive and active processes. Use scenarios involving concentration gradients and polarity/size of molecules.
anti_prompts: Ensure students distinguish between channel and carrier proteins in facilitated diffusion.

subtopics.name: Binary Fission and the Eukaryotic Cell Cycle

technique_notes.core_concepts: Asexual reproduction in prokaryotes. The eukaryotic cycle: Interphase (G1, S, G2), Mitosis (PMAT), and Cytokinesis.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Focus on the purpose of each stage and the significance of producing genetically identical daughter cells.
anti_prompts: Do not skip the G0 resting phase or the checkpoints in your descriptions.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Programmed cell death. Pathways (intrinsic/extrinsic). Role of caspases. Connection to cancer and deviant cell behavior.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how a failure in apoptosis leads to uncontrolled cell division. Describe the characteristics of cancer cells.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse apoptosis with necrosis (unregulated cell death).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Properties of stem cells: self-renewal and potency (totipotency, pluripotency, multipotency). Differentiation into specialised tissues.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Compare different levels of potency and their potential applications in medicine.
anti_prompts: Ensure clear distinction between pluripotent and multipotent cells.

subtopics.name: Specialisation and Organisation in Vascular Plants

technique_notes.core_concepts: Xylem and phloem. Tissues for water intake, movement, and loss (stomata).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Link the structure of vascular tissues to their specific roles in transpiration and translocation.
anti_prompts: Avoid naming every plant cell type; focus on the transport systems.

subtopics.name: Animal Systems: Digestive, Endocrine and Excretory

technique_notes.core_concepts: Organisation of cells into tissues and organs. Basic functions of the three systems mentioned in the Study Design.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Focus on how these systems contribute to overall organism function and interact with each other.
anti_prompts: Do not require detailed anatomy of systems not listed (e.g., skeletal).

subtopics.name: Regulation of Water Balance in Plants

technique_notes.core_concepts: Transpiration, stomatal control, and adaptations to dry environments (xerophytes).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the factors affecting transpiration rate and how plants minimize water loss.
anti_prompts: Avoid generic 'saving water' answers; use physiological mechanisms.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Negative feedback loops. Components: stimulus, receptor, modulator, effector, response.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Require students to map out loops for temperature, blood glucose, and water balance in animals.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse the effector with the response.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Type 1 diabetes, hypoglycaemia, and hyperthyroidism.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the physiological basis for the malfunction and the resulting symptoms/impact on the organism.
anti_prompts: Ensure distinction between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes (Type 2 is not the focus here).

subtopics.name: Meiosis and the Production of Gametes

technique_notes.core_concepts: Key events in Meiosis I and II. Crossing over, independent assortment, and non-disjunction.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Emphasise how meiosis leads to genetic variation in offspring. Compare with mitosis.
anti_prompts: Do not forget that DNA replication occurs *before* meiosis starts.

technique_notes.core_concepts: The nature of chromosomes. Relationship between DNA, genes, and alleles. The concept of a genome.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Distinguish between genotype and phenotype. Use correct terminology for homozygous and heterozygous individuals.
anti_prompts: Avoid confusing a gene locus with an allele.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Dominance, co-dominance, incomplete dominance, and sex-linkage.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Predict outcomes of monohybrid and dihybrid genetic crosses using Punnett squares. Calculate probabilities.
anti_prompts: Ensure students use consistent and clear allele symbols.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Interpretation of pedigree charts to determine the mode of inheritance (autosomal vs sex-linked, dominant vs recessive).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Require students to justify their determined mode using specific individuals from the chart.
anti_prompts: Do not use non-VCAA symbols; ensure gender symbols are correct.

technique_notes.core_concepts: How external factors and epigenetic modifications (e.g., DNA methylation) influence phenotypic expression without changing the DNA sequence.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use examples like agouti mice or twins to explain the interaction between genes and environment.
anti_prompts: Do not imply that epigenetic changes are always permanent or easily reversible.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Advantages and disadvantages of reproductive strategies. Types of asexual reproduction (budding, fragmentation, etc.).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Compare the survival value of each strategy in stable vs changing environments.
anti_prompts: Do not state that asexual reproduction is 'easier' without biological justification.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) and embryo splitting in animals. Plant tissue culture.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe the steps involved in SCNT and evaluate the ethical implications and biological impacts.
anti_prompts: Ensure students distinguish between the donor of the nucleus and the surrogate mother.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Structural, physiological, and behavioral adaptations. Survival value in specific Australian ecosystems.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Link adaptations directly to an environmental challenge (e.g., heat, aridity).
anti_prompts: Avoid anthropomorphism; organisms don't 'choose' to adapt.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Symbiosis (mutualism, commensalism, parasitism). Keystone species and top predators.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how the removal of a keystone species impacts the entire ecosystem structure.
anti_prompts: Do not list relationships without explaining their ecological impact.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander knowledge in understanding and managing survival in ecosystems.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Respectfully integrate Indigenous knowledge about species interdependencies and land management.
anti_prompts: Avoid tokenistic inclusion; link knowledge to specific ecological concepts.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Ethical concepts: integrity, justice, beneficence, non-maleficence, respect. Ethical approaches: consequentialism, duty-based, virtue-based.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Apply frameworks to contemporary issues (e.g., genetic testing, cloning). Require multi-perspective evaluation.
anti_prompts: Do not ask for personal opinion; require structured ethical analysis.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Nucleotides as monomers. Double helix DNA vs single-stranded RNA. mRNA, tRNA, rRNA roles.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Compare the sugar, bases, and general structure of DNA and the three types of RNA.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse the roles of the three RNA types.

subtopics.name: The Genetic Code and Gene Expression

technique_notes.core_concepts: Universal triplet code. Degeneracy/redundancy. Transcription (initiation, elongation, termination) and Translation.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe the steps of transcription and translation. Explain why the code is degenerate and how this protects against some mutations.
anti_prompts: Ensure students mention RNA polymerase and ribosomes in their descriptions.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Addition of 5' methyl cap and 3' poly-A tail. Splicing of introns and joining of exons. Alternative splicing.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how alternative splicing allows one gene to code for multiple proteins.
anti_prompts: Do not imply that RNA processing occurs in prokaryotes.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Exons, introns, promoter, and operator regions. Trp operon in bacteria as an example of gene regulation.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how the trp operon is regulated by repression and attenuation. Link to energy conservation.
anti_prompts: Avoid overly detailed attenuation steps; focus on the regulatory logic.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Amino acids, polypeptide chains. Hierarchical levels: primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary structures.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how the primary sequence determines the final 3D shape and function of a protein. Define the proteome.
anti_prompts: Do not just list the levels; explain the bonds/interactions at each level.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Role of ribosomes, rough ER, Golgi apparatus, and transport/secretory vesicles.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Trace the path of a protein from synthesis to secretion. Distinguish between membrane-bound and free ribosomes.
anti_prompts: Ensure students mention the role of the Golgi in modification and packaging.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Polymerases (synthesis), ligases (joining), and endonucleases (restriction enzymes/cutting).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how restriction enzymes create sticky or blunt ends and the significance for recombinant DNA.
anti_prompts: Ensure students know that ligase joins the sugar-phosphate backbone.

subtopics.name: CRISPR-Cas9 in Bacteria and Genome Editing

technique_notes.core_concepts: Natural role as an adaptive immune system in bacteria. Application as a tool for targeted gene editing using gRNA.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe the components (Cas9 enzyme and guide RNA) and the process of locating and cutting DNA.
anti_prompts: Avoid over-complicating the PAM sequence; focus on the gRNA targeting.

technique_notes.core_concepts: PCR: denaturation, annealing, extension. Gel: sorting fragments by size using charge and a porous matrix.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret gel runs for DNA profiling or genetic screening. Explain the role of Taq polymerase.
anti_prompts: Ensure students understand that smaller fragments move further/faster in the gel.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Plasmids as vectors. Transformation process. Use of antibiotic resistance genes for selection.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe the steps to produce human insulin using transformed bacteria.
anti_prompts: Do not forget to mention the role of the heat shock or electroporation step.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Difference between GMOs and transgenic organisms. Applications in agriculture (productivity, disease resistance).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Evaluate the biological, social, and ethical implications of using GMs in food production.
anti_prompts: Ensure distinction between 'genetically modified' (general) and 'transgenic' (genes from different species).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Enzymes as biological catalysts. Active site and substrate specificity. Role of coenzymes (ATP, NADH, NADPH) as cycling carriers.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the cycling of coenzymes between loaded and unloaded states in biochemical pathways.
anti_prompts: Avoid confusing coenzymes with inorganic cofactors.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Temperature, pH, substrate/enzyme concentration. Competitive and non-competitive inhibitors.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Interpret graphs of enzyme activity. Explain denaturation at high temps/extreme pH. Distinguish between inhibitor types.
anti_prompts: Ensure students explain the *mechanism* of inhibition (e.g., binding to active site vs allosteric site).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Location: thylakoid membranes. Inputs: water, light, NADP+, ADP+Pi. Outputs: oxygen, NADPH, ATP.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Focus on the photolysis of water and the role of chlorophyll in trapping light energy.
anti_prompts: Do not require the names of specific photosystems or electron carriers.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Location: stroma. Inputs: CO2, NADPH, ATP. Outputs: glucose, NADP+, ADP+Pi, water.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how the products of the first stage are used to fix carbon into organic molecules.
anti_prompts: Details of the Calvin Cycle intermediates are not required.

subtopics.name: Rubisco and C3, C4, CAM Adaptations

technique_notes.core_concepts: The role of Rubisco in carbon fixation. The problem of photorespiration. Adaptations to maximize efficiency.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Compare how C4 and CAM plants minimize photorespiration through spatial or temporal separation of CO2 fixation.
anti_prompts: Avoid overly detailed biochemical differences; focus on the ecological advantage.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Location: cytosol. Inputs: glucose, NAD+, ADP+Pi. Outputs: pyruvate, NADH, 2 ATP.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain that glycolysis occurs in both aerobic and anaerobic pathways.
anti_prompts: Details of the 10 enzyme-catalyzed steps are not required.

subtopics.name: Aerobic Respiration: Krebs Cycle and ETC

technique_notes.core_concepts: Krebs: matrix, produces CO2, NADH, FADH2, 2 ATP. ETC: cristae, produces water and ~26-28 ATP using oxygen as terminal acceptor.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Link the high ATP yield of aerobic respiration to the role of oxygen and the structure of the mitochondria.
anti_prompts: Intermediate steps of Krebs are out of scope.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Location: cytosol. Differences in outputs between animals (lactic acid) and yeasts (ethanol + CO2).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the purpose of fermentation: to regenerate NAD+ so glycolysis can continue.
anti_prompts: Ensure students note the significantly lower ATP yield compared to aerobic respiration.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Use of CRISPR-Cas9 to improve photosynthetic efficiency. Anaerobic fermentation of biomass for biofuel production.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Evaluate how these biotechnologies address global challenges like food security and sustainable energy.
anti_prompts: Avoid generic sustainability answers; link to the specific biological pathway.

subtopics.name: Physical and Chemical Barriers in Plants and Animals

technique_notes.core_concepts: Innate immunity: first line of defense. Barriers like skin, mucus, stomach acid (animals) and waxy cuticles, galls (plants).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Categorise barriers as physical, chemical, or microbiota.
anti_prompts: Do not forget that plants also have an active innate response (though barriers are the focus here).

subtopics.name: The Innate Immune Response in Animals

technique_notes.core_concepts: Non-specific response. Role of phagocytes, NK cells, mast cells (histamine), and the inflammatory response.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe the steps of the inflammatory response and the role of complement proteins and interferons.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse the roles of different leukocytes.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Self vs non-self. MHC I (all nucleated cells) and MHC II (antigen-presenting cells) markers.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how the immune system distinguishes between host cells and pathogens. Define allergens as a type of antigen.
anti_prompts: Ensure students understand that MHC markers are proteins.

technique_notes.core_concepts: B cells, plasma cells, and antibodies. Memory B cells for long-term immunity.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the process of clonal selection and expansion. Describe antibody structure and function (neutralisation, opsonisation).
anti_prompts: Do not confuse antibodies with antigens.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Antigen-presenting cells (dendritic cells). T helper cells and Cytotoxic T cells. Memory T cells.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe how Tc cells identify and destroy infected or abnormal cells. Explain the role of Th cells in coordinating the response.
anti_prompts: Ensure students distinguish between the roles of B cells and T cells.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Role in transporting APCs and lymphocytes. Primary (bone marrow, thymus) and secondary (lymph nodes, spleen) lymphoid tissues.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how the lymphatic system facilitates the adaptive immune response by bringing antigens to lymph nodes.
anti_prompts: Do not overlook the role of the lymphatic system in drainage and transport.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Natural vs artificial, active vs passive immunity. The role of booster shots.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how vaccines induce a primary immune response to create memory cells. Compare with passive treatments like antivenoms.
anti_prompts: Ensure clear distinction between active and passive acquired immunity.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Concept of herd immunity and the required vaccination threshold. Pathogens: viruses, bacteria, fungi, protozoa, prions.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Analyze the impact of varying vaccination rates on population protection. Distinguish between epidemic and pandemic.
anti_prompts: Do not imply that herd immunity protects individuals; it protects the population.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Production of mAbs. Application in treating cancer (targeted delivery, blocking signals) and autoimmune diseases.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the mechanism of action for a specific mAb application. Evaluate the ethical considerations.
anti_prompts: Ensure students know that mAbs are specific to a single antigen.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Natural selection, gene flow, genetic drift (bottleneck and founder effects). Mutations as the source of new alleles.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Apply the 4-step natural selection model to a scenario. Explain how drift reduces genetic diversity in small populations.
anti_prompts: Avoid Lamarckian language; use 'selection pressure' and 'selective advantage'.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Isolation mechanisms (pre-zygotic and post-zygotic). Role of geographical and reproductive barriers.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Describe the process of speciation following the isolation of populations. Use examples like Galápagos finches or Howea palms.
anti_prompts: Ensure students mention the lack of gene flow between populations.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Fossil record, transitional fossils. Relative dating (stratigraphy, index fossils) and absolute dating (radiometric).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain the conditions required for fossilisation. Compare dating methods and their limitations.
anti_prompts: Ensure students know that radiometric dating is for the surrounding rock, not usually the fossil itself.

technique_notes.core_concepts: DNA and amino acid sequence comparisons. Conserved genes (e.g., cytochrome c). Molecular clocks.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Use sequence alignment data to determine the time since divergence and construct phylogenetic trees.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse molecular homology with structural homology.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Interpreting relationships from trees. Identifying common ancestors, nodes, and branches.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Determine the most closely related species based on the branching pattern. Explain the impact of new data on tree structure.
anti_prompts: Do not assume that species at the 'tips' are direct ancestors of each other.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Trends in skull anatomy (brain size, brow ridges, teeth). Transition to bipedalism. Significant species (Australopithecus, H. habilis, H. erectus, H. neanderthalensis).
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify trends in the hominin fossil record. Compare structural features of early and modern hominins.
anti_prompts: Ensure distinction between 'hominid' (all great apes) and 'hominin' (human lineage).

technique_notes.core_concepts: Tool use, fire, and symbolic art. Impact on diet and social structure. Interbreeding between Homo sapiens, Neanderthals, and Denisovans.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Explain how technological advancements influenced biological evolution (e.g., fire and smaller jaw/teeth). Evaluate evidence for interbreeding.
anti_prompts: Avoid stating that evolution is a linear progress; it's a branching bush.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Selective breeding (artificial selection). Impact on genetic diversity and health of populations.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Compare natural selection with artificial selection. Discuss the ethics and biological consequences of intensive selective breeding.
anti_prompts: Ensure students note that humans are the selection pressure in artificial selection.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Independent, dependent, and controlled variables. Hypotheses and research questions.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify variables in a given scenario. Evaluate the validity of an experimental design based on the control of variables.
anti_prompts: Ensure students distinguish between the control group and controlled variables.

technique_notes.core_concepts: Qualitative vs quantitative data. Accuracy, precision, repeatability, and reproducibility. Random and systematic errors.
technique_notes.exam_style_guidelines: Identify sources of error in an experiment and suggest improvements. Distinguish between outliers and uncertainties.
anti_prompts: Do not confuse accuracy with precision.
